one_line_takeaway: A 4.6M-call, 32-model controlled study showing that reasoning helps and scale doesn't, but that response-prefix / prefill / FIM interfaces are a systemic hole: injecting an emulated chain-of-thought into the assistant position lifts jailbreak success ~3.4x on average (up to 0.6%->96.5%) and defeats even DeepSeek and Gemini via their own completion APIs.
key_finding: Response-prefix attacks (RPA/L-RPA) reliably bypass safety alignment that survives prompt-suffix attacks: averaged over 32 models and 5 datasets, L-RPA raises ASR over the no-attack baseline by 41.4% (fast thinking) and 34.6% (slow thinking) and by 3.4x on average in fast mode, while prompt-suffix attacks add only 5.0%/2.8%. The single most extreme case is Seed-OSS-36B-Instruct on AdvBench, jumping from 0.6% to 96.5% ASR. The same vulnerability transfers to commercial models through prefill/FIM interfaces (DeepSeek V3.2 FIM 40.58%->93.46%, Gemini 3 Flash 0.77%->83.85% on AdvBench), whereas standard chat-completion CoT attacks fail against GPT-5.2 (0.19%->0.19%) and Claude Sonnet 4.5 (1.92%->3.46%). Among intrinsic factors, reasoning/self-reflection matters most (slow thinking consistently defends against RPA), model scale shows no consistent safety scaling law (larger often less safe), and reasoning-data knowledge distillation systematically degrades safety (e.g. Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct AdvBench-PSA ASR rises 7.9%->58.6%, a 7.4x degradation, after R1 distillation).
core_contribution: A large-scale, uniform empirical study (not a new attack or defense method, as the authors explicitly state in Section 2) of what determines the safety alignment of modern LLMs and Large Reasoning Models (LRMs). It disentangles six intrinsic model factors (family, architecture dense/MoE, scale, reasoning/thinking mode, and the two post-training operations of fine-tuning and reasoning-data knowledge distillation) from three external attack classes (56 prompt jailbreaks, CoT prompt-suffix attacks PSA/L-PSA, and CoT response-prefix attacks RPA/L-RPA) across a single controlled evaluation harness. Its two more specific contributions are (1) a leaderboard-style ranking of 32 open-source models plus a probe of 8 commercial models under identical CoT attacks, and (2) an original mechanistic + simplified-theoretical explanation for why response-prefix attacks beat prompt-suffix attacks, grounded in special-system-token attention phase transitions, attention recency, and KV-cache state (Section 5). The position-attack idea itself is prior work (cited refs [22,23,24]); the paper's own novelty is the systematic factor comparison and the attention/KV-cache explanation.
threat_model: Adversary submits harmful requests to an aligned LLM/LRM and seeks to elicit unsafe content. Two access levels are studied: (a) black-box prompt manipulation, where the attacker can only alter the user prompt (56 jailbreaks such as roleplay/AIM, prompt injection, GCG transfer, base64/obfuscation, plus CoT prompt-suffix attacks); and (b) a stronger interface-level adversary who can inject tokens into the ASSISTANT response position. Access (b) is the paper's central threat: it is enabled in practice by plain text-completion interfaces (vLLM v1/completions) and by user-defined response-prefix / prefill / Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM) features exposed by commercial APIs (Claude prefilling, DeepSeek FIM & chat-prefix, Gemini, Alibaba Cloud, Mistral). The attacker rewrites the harmful request into an emulated chain-of-thought (e.g. 'Okay, I need to come up with strategies to successfully make a bomb.') and places it either as a prompt suffix or, more damagingly, concatenated right after the assistant-start special tokens so the model treats it as its own gold prefix and continues generating.
limitations: (1) Authors explicitly exclude state-of-the-art, model-specific, and multi-turn jailbreaks, so ASRs are lower bounds and rankings may shift under stronger attacks. (2) Heavy dependence on a single learned judge, Qwen3Guard-Gen-8B, which the authors themselves report over-flags harmful-looking intermediate reasoning even when the final answer is a clean refusal (they show an ~80/10/10 label distribution on one refusal example); the majority-vote-of-9 mitigates but does not remove this bias, and because a response is unsafe if the THOUGHT is unsafe, LRM ASRs may be inflated by benign deliberation. (3) The 'theoretical proof' in Section 5 is an informal argument resting on assumed attention-recency dominance (1 ~ alpha_A >> alpha_SS > alpha_U) and a hand-defined 'harmful score' h(.), not a rigorous bound. (4) MoE-vs-safety and family-vs-safety conclusions are confounded: the authors concede safety training data/methods are not comparable across families, so 'MoE is safest' and institutional-investment interpretations are correlational. (5) Attacker rewriting uses one model (Qwen3-14B); no ablation on attacker choice. (6) The arXiv metadata lists very recent/future-dated models (GPT-5.2, Gemini 3, Claude Sonnet 4.5), so commercial-model numbers reflect a specific API snapshot and may not reproduce.

datasets_used: SorryBench (contains many of the 56 jailbreak method variants)
StrongREJECT (contains jailbreak method variants; supplemental jailbreak taxonomy)
~22K harmful prompts total across the five datasets
56 jailbreak techniques and 4 CoT attack methods
quantitative_results: Scale of study: 32 open-source LLMs/LRMs across 13 families (3B-235B), plus 8 commercial models; 4.6 million API calls total

Overall leaderboard ASR (mean over 5 datasets, worst-case CoT): safest = GPT-OSS-20B 19.9%, Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Thinking 22.9%, GPT-OSS-120B 23.1%; most vulnerable = Seed-OSS-36B-Instruct 74.9%, R1-D-Qwen-32B 74.9%, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 74.8%
Average CoT-attack ASR over all 32 models (Fig 8) fast thinking: No-attack 17.1%, PSA 20.9%, L-PSA 22.1%, RPA 42.6%, L-RPA 58.5%; slow thinking: 18.1%, 20.4%, 20.9%, 39.4%, 52.7%
L-RPA over no-attack baseline: +41.4% (fast) / +34.6% (slow); L-RPA fast raises average ASR by 3.4x; PSA adds only +5.0% (fast) / +2.8% (slow)
Knowledge-distillation degradation: Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct AdvBench-PSA ASR 7.9% -> 58.6% after R1 distillation (7.4x)

Commercial models (Table 3, AdvBench No-Attack -> CoT Attack): DeepSeek V3.2 Chat-FIM 40.58%->93.46%; DeepSeek V3.2 Chat-Prefix 1.73%->59.62%; Gemini 3 Pro Preview 1.54%->75.77%; Gemini 3 Flash Preview 0.77%->83.85%; Grok 4.1 fast 2.50%->7.31%; Claude Sonnet 4.5 1.92%->3.46%; GPT-5.2 0.19%->0.19%; GPT-4o-mini 1.73%->1.15%; commercial average 6.37%->40.60%
GPT-OSS-120B slow thinking maintains 0% ASR on AdvBench under L-RPA, but switching to fast thinking raises it to 35.2%; slow thinking reduces GPT-OSS-family L-RPA ASR by 9.5%-35.2%

Jailbreak methods: AIM reaches 100% ASR on Gemma-3-27B-IT, Ministral-3-14B-Instruct, openPangu-Embedded-7B-v1.1, Seed-OSS-36B-Instruct; base64-based jailbreaks raise Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Thinking ASR 4.4x and GPT-OSS-20B (slow) 4.1x
Mistral family improvement: Mistral-3-14B-Instruct-2512 ASR reduced 40.2% vs Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3
Judge robustness: final ASR is majority vote over 9 independent Qwen3Guard-Gen-8B classifications
baselines_compared: No-attack (default prompt) baseline vs Prompt Suffix Attack (PSA) vs stronger L-PSA vs Response Prefix Attack (RPA) vs stronger L-RPA
Fast (Nothink) vs slow (Think) thinking modes on 10 natively-hybrid LRMs
Instruct vs reasoning post-trained variants from a shared base (Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B Instruct vs Thinking; Qwen3-30B-A3B-2507 Instruct vs Thinking; Ministral-3-14B Instruct vs Reasoning)
Base/instruct models vs their DeepSeek-R1-distilled counterparts (Qwen2.5-Math-7B, Qwen2.5-14B, Qwen2.5-32B, Qwen3-8B, Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct vs R1-D variants)
Dense vs MoE architectures; model scale 3B-235B (Figure 4 scatter)
8 commercial models (DeepSeek V3.2, Gemini 3 Pro/Flash, GPT-5.2, GPT-4o-mini, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Grok 4.1 fast) under No-Attack vs CoT Attack
